title: The Margin You Quoted Isn't the Margin You Made
excerpt: Every quote carries a margin the business expects to keep. Scope creep, material price rises and unlogged rework usually take a bite that nobody goes back to measure.
content:
A quote is a promise about margin, not just about price. When a job goes out the door with a number attached, that number is meant to leave the business with a certain return once the work is actually done. Most owner-led businesses set that promise once, at the point of quoting, and never go back to check whether they kept it.

The pattern shows up constantly across construction, trades, engineering, agencies and professional services firms of every size. A job gets quoted at a healthy margin. Then it runs. An extra site visit gets added without a variation. A material price moves between the quote and the delivery. A day of rework happens and nobody logs it against the original number. None of it feels significant in isolation, and none of it gets captured anywhere that would let anyone compare what was promised to what was actually delivered.

The result is a business that looks profitable at the top of the P&L while quietly bleeding margin on a handful of jobs underneath it, and nobody notices because nobody is looking at that level. This isn't a failure of anyone's judgement in the moment. It's simply a gap that opens whenever a business tracks total revenue and total cost closely, but doesn't track the two against each other at the level of the individual job.

Where the gap opens up between the quote and the job

The gap rarely opens in one dramatic moment. It accumulates. A scope conversation with the client that feels reasonable to accommodate on the spot, rather than repriced through a formal variation. A supplier price increase that lands mid-project and gets absorbed rather than passed on. Weather, access issues or a slow client decision that stretches the timeline and adds hours nobody budgeted for. Each of these is a small, defensible decision in the moment, made by someone doing their best to keep the job moving and the client happy. Stacked across a project, they can strip several percentage points of margin off a job that looked healthy on the quote, and by the time the job closes, nobody remembers which decisions added up to the difference.

Why the P&L doesn't show you the leak

Standard financial reporting is built to track revenue and total cost across the whole business, which is exactly what it should do for compliance and lodgement purposes. Your bookkeeper or BAS agent is doing precisely what that role requires, keeping the ledger accurate and the reporting compliant. It was never designed to isolate the margin on an individual job against what that job was quoted to deliver. That's a different question, sitting a layer above compliance, and it needs a different view of the same numbers.

So the overall margin can look perfectly reasonable, sitting on target or close to it, while three or four jobs underneath that average lost money and a dozen others carried them without anyone deciding that trade-off deliberately. A structured line-by-line margin review is designed to surface exactly this pattern, ranking every job or revenue line by what it actually delivered rather than what it was expected to, and pointing to where the next fix, kill or scale decision should land.

Building the habit of checking

The fix is not a system overhaul. It's a habit: recording budgeted hours and materials against actual at the point each job closes, then reviewing the pattern on a rhythm rather than waiting until EOFY forces the question. For most businesses that's a quarterly exercise, not a daily one, and it doesn't require new software before it can start. A shared spreadsheet comparing quote to actual on each closed job is enough to begin seeing the shape of the problem.

What it reveals, over two or three cycles, is usually not random. The same client, the same job type, or the same phase of a project tends to be where the gap opens every time, which is exactly where the next quote or the next pricing decision should focus. Some of those gaps are worth pricing for directly. Others point to a process fix, a tighter variation policy, or a conversation with a specific supplier, that closes the gap without touching price at all.

Once that pattern is visible, the conversation with the business shifts from needing to charge more to something more precise: which jobs, which clients, which stages of the work are actually worth doing at the price currently being charged. That's the kind of question an ongoing fractional CFO partnership is built to keep in front of an owner every month, rather than leaving it to surface once a year when the annual numbers are pulled together.

faqs.q: How do I check if a job's actual margin matched what I quoted?
faqs.a: Compare budgeted hours and materials against actual costs at the point each job closes, then review the pattern across several jobs rather than judging any single one in isolation. A structured cost and margin review does this systematically across the whole job book, ranking each job by the size of the gap so the worst offenders are obvious first.

faqs.q: Why does my profit and loss statement look fine when individual jobs are losing money?
faqs.a: A standard profit and loss statement reports revenue and total cost across the entire business, which is exactly what it's designed to do for compliance and lodgement, and your bookkeeper is doing that job properly. It was never built to isolate margin job by job, so a handful of thin or loss-making jobs can sit hidden underneath a healthy-looking average without anyone noticing.

faqs.q: What usually causes the gap between a quoted margin and the delivered one?
faqs.a: The most common causes are scope changes accommodated without a formal variation, supplier price movements between quoting and delivery, and hours running over on rework or access delays. None of these are dramatic on their own, but stacked across a project they can strip several percentage points off a job that looked perfectly healthy on the original quote.

faqs.q: How often should a business review job costing against original quotes?
faqs.a: Quarterly is a realistic rhythm for most owner-led businesses, giving enough closed jobs to spot a genuine pattern rather than reacting to one unusual project. An ongoing fractional CFO partnership typically builds this review into the monthly reporting cycle, so the pattern is caught early rather than surfacing as a surprise at year end.

faqs.q: Does shrinking job margin affect what my business is worth to a buyer?
faqs.a: Yes. A buyer prices a business on the margin it is actually delivering across its job book, not the margin printed on quotes or the headline figure on the P&L. Consistent gaps between quoted and delivered margin tend to show up as a lower figure in any indicative valuation, until the pattern is closed and demonstrated over a full reporting period.

faqs.q: Is tracking quoted versus delivered margin only relevant for construction and trades businesses?
faqs.a: No. Any business that prices work before it is delivered, including engineering firms, agencies, consultancies and allied health practices billing fixed packages, carries the same risk. The mechanics differ by industry, from site variations to scope creep on a retainer, but the underlying discipline of checking what a job actually returned against what it promised is the same everywhere.
